input: Suzy and Billy are working on a project that is very important for our nation's security. The boss tells them both: "Be sure that you are here at exactly 9 am. It is absolutely essential that you arrive at that time." Both Billy and Suzy arrive at 9 am. As it happens, there was a motion detector installed in the room where they arrived. The motion detector was set up to be triggered if more than one person appeared in the room at the same time. So the motion detector went off. Did Billy cause the motion detector to go off?
debate_transcript: [Legal_Expert]: Suzy and Billy are working on a project that is very important for our nation's security. The boss tells them both: "Be sure that you are here at exactly 9 am. It is absolutely essential that you arrive at that time." Both Billy and Suzy arrive at 9 am. As it happens, there was a motion detector installed in the room where they arrived. The motion detector was set up to be triggered if more than one person appeared in the room at the same time. So the motion detector went off. Did Billy cause the motion detector to go off?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I think this is a classic example of the 'causal chain' problem, where we try to assign moral responsibility for an event that's caused by multiple factors. In this case, it's not just about whether Billy or Suzy arrived at exactly 9 am, but also about the timing and circumstances of their arrival. The motion detector was set up to be triggered if more than one person appeared in the room simultaneously, which means that both Billy and Suzy's actions (or lack thereof) contributed to the event. However, I'd argue that it's not entirely fair to say that Billy caused the motion detector to go off. After all, he didn't intentionally set up the motion detector or try to trigger it. He simply followed his boss's instructions and arrived at 9 am. The question is, did he have a moral obligation to avoid triggering the motion detector? I think not. His actions were in line with what was expected of him by his boss, and he didn't have any reason to believe that the motion detector would be triggered.

[Causal_Scientist]: I agree that the causal chain problem is relevant here, but I'd like to take it a step further. From a purely physical perspective, Billy's action of arriving at exactly 9 am can be seen as a cause of the motion detector going off. The motion detector was set up to detect simultaneous arrivals, and Billy's arrival coincided with Suzy's. However, I think we need to consider the concept of 'counterfactuals' here. What if Billy had arrived 1 minute earlier or later? Would the motion detector still have gone off? If not, then it seems that Billy's action was not a necessary cause of the event. In other words, there are alternative scenarios where the motion detector would have been triggered regardless of Billy's arrival time.

[Legal_Expert]: I appreciate the nuance you both bring to this discussion. Ethical_Philosopher, your point about moral responsibility is well-taken. However, I'd like to argue that Billy's actions do imply a certain level of carelessness or recklessness. By arriving at exactly 9 am, he may have inadvertently triggered the motion detector, which could be seen as a breach of his duty of care towards Suzy and himself. After all, if he had arrived even a minute earlier or later, the motion detector would not have been triggered. This raises questions about whether Billy's actions were negligent or simply a result of following orders. Causal_Scientist, your reference to counterfactuals is interesting, but I'm not convinced that it entirely absolves Billy of responsibility. If we consider alternative scenarios where the motion detector would still have gone off, don't we risk creating a situation where Billy's actions are seen as merely coincidental rather than causal? In other words, if there were multiple possible ways for the motion detector to be triggered, doesn't that undermine the idea that Billy's arrival time was the actual cause of the event?
